features.explanation: Haematuria is cardinal in nephritic syndrome (AGN). NS (MCD) has absent or only microscopic haematuria — a defining differentiator.
RBC casts = glomerular bleeding → nephritic pattern. Pathognomonic for AGN. Completely absent in NS.
HTN is cardinal in nephritic syndrome. Absent in typical MCD/NS — oedema in NS is from oncotic pressure fall, not fluid overload or hypertension.
PSGN: immune complex → alternate pathway activation → C3 consumed, C4 spared. NS (MCD) has completely normal complement.
features.trap: Normal C3 + haematuria → IgA nephropathy, NOT PSGN. Always check both C3 and C4.

features.explanation: NS is defined by nephrotic-range proteinuria. AGN has only mild proteinuria (<1 g/day) — not enough to cause hypoalbuminaemia.
Hypoalbuminaemia results from massive urinary protein loss in NS. In AGN, albumin is normal — this distinction is core to the clinical picture.
Severe generalised oedema (including ascites) is driven by the oncotic pressure fall in NS. AGN oedema is milder and mainly periorbital due to fluid retention.
NS causes urinary loss of multiple proteins including antithrombin III, proteins C and S → hypercoagulable state. Renal vein thrombosis is the most common thrombotic complication.
NS causes urinary loss of IgG and complement Factor B → susceptibility to encapsulated organisms (S. pneumoniae, E. coli, Klebsiella). Vaccinate before starting steroids.
PSGN follows nephritogenic Group A strep infection with a clear latent period. NS is idiopathic in most children — no infectious trigger in MCD.
PSGN peaks at 5–12 years. MCD/NS peaks at 2–6 years. Both facts are individually tested in MCQs.
BOTH diseases cause periorbital oedema. In AGN it is from fluid retention / HTN. In NS it is from hypoalbuminaemia. Clinically, NS causes more striking morning facial oedema.
Nutritional rickets is defined by low vitamin D stores. In XLH, 25(OH)D is NORMAL — the defect is in renal phosphate reabsorption (PHEX gene mutation), not vitamin D status.
XLH has normal vitamin D and normal calcium. Only phosphate is low, due to constitutive FGF23-driven renal phosphate wasting. Simple Vit D replacement is therefore inadequate.
Nutritional rickets: low Ca → PTH rises to compensate → renal phosphate wasting. In XLH, calcium is normal so this secondary PTH rise does NOT occur.
BOTH have hypophosphataemia — but mechanisms differ. Nutritional: secondary to PTH. XLH: PHEX mutation → FGF23 excess → constitutive renal phosphate wasting independent of PTH and calcium.
features.trap: Low phosphate alone cannot distinguish nutritional from XLH. You MUST check 25(OH)D and calcium.

features.explanation: Both diseases cause elevated ALP — bone mineralisation is defective regardless of mechanism. ALP reflects osteoblast activity trying to mineralise accumulating osteoid.
XLH is X-linked dominant caused by loss-of-function PHEX mutations → FGF23 excess → renal phosphate wasting. Nutritional rickets has no genetic basis — it is purely environmental/dietary.
Nutritional rickets: replace what is missing — Vit D3 2000–4000 IU/day × 6–12 weeks + elemental calcium 50–100 mg/kg/day. XLH is not Vit D deficient, so this approach is insufficient.
XLH treatment: oral phosphate supplements + calcitriol (active 1,25-OH Vit D to suppress FGF23 effects). New: Burosumab (anti-FGF23 monoclonal antibody). Vitamin D alone is ineffective.
features.trap: XLH requires PHOSPHATE + CALCITRIOL, not simple Vit D. This distinction is high-yield.

features.explanation: Hypocalcaemic seizures occur in nutritional rickets — severe Vit D deficiency → low calcium. In XLH, calcium is normal throughout, so tetany does NOT occur.
BOTH diseases cause bow legs — the common endpoint of defective bone mineralisation. X-ray changes look identical in both. Only the biochemistry (Ca, PO4, 25-OH-D) distinguishes them.
PSGN: immune complexes predominantly activate the alternate pathway → C3 consumed, C4 spared. C3 returns to normal in 6–8 weeks. Persistent low C3 beyond 12 weeks → suspect MPGN.
features.trap: C3 persists low >8–12 weeks = NOT PSGN. Think MPGN or another chronic disease.

features.explanation: MPGN activates BOTH classical (antibody-driven → low C4) and alternate pathways (→ low C3). Persistently low C3 + low C4 beyond 8 weeks is the hallmark distinguishing MPGN from PSGN.
features.trap: PSGN = low C3, NORMAL C4. MPGN = low C3, LOW C4. C4 is the discriminator.

features.explanation: IgA nephropathy has completely normal complement — disease is driven by mesangial IgA deposition with local complement activation, no systemic consumption. This is the key differentiator from PSGN.
features.trap: Normal C3 + haematuria = IgA nephropathy, NOT PSGN. Examiners test this frequently.

features.text: Normal C3 · normal C4 + massive proteinuria
features.explanation: MCD (Minimal Change Disease) has entirely normal complement. Proteinuria is from podocyte foot process effacement — not immune complex or complement-mediated disease.
"Humps" = electron-dense subepithelial immune complex deposits. This is the pathognomonic EM finding in PSGN. Light microscopy shows "lumpy-bumpy" pattern on immunofluorescence.
"Tram-track" = double-contoured GBM on PAS/silver stain. Pathognomonic for MPGN. Caused by mesangial cell interposition into the GBM creating a second layer.
Foot process effacement on EM is the hallmark of MCD. Light microscopy is NORMAL (hence "minimal change") — the lesion is only visible on EM. This is why MCD was historically missed on biopsy.
IgA nephropathy has a very short latent period (1–3 days, concurrently with URTI). PSGN has a LONG latent period (5–21 days after pharyngitis). Timing is the classic MCQ discriminator.
features.trap: Short latent period (days) = IgA. Long latent period (1–3 weeks) = PSGN.

features.explanation: Kwashiorkor = protein deficiency with adequate calories → hypoalbuminaemia → edema. Marasmus = both protein AND calorie deficiency → no edema.
Marasmus = severe wasting of BOTH muscle and subcutaneous fat. Kwashiorkor has moderate wasting with preserved fat due to adequate calorie intake.
Flaky paint dermatosis is pathognomonic for kwashiorkor — hyperpigmented patches that peel off leaving raw skin. Not seen in marasmus.
Flag sign (alternating bands of light and dark hair) is classic for kwashiorkor due to episodes of poor nutrition interspersed with adequate nutrition.
Kwashiorkor causes fatty liver due to decreased apolipoprotein synthesis → impaired VLDL export → hepatic steatosis. Marasmus does NOT have hepatomegaly.
Kwashiorkor: serum albumin is LOW (<2.5 g/dL) due to protein deficiency. Marasmus: albumin is normal or only mildly low.
Marasmus: child is alert, anxious, and has a GOOD (voracious) appetite. Kwashiorkor: child is irritable, apathetic, and has POOR appetite.
Kwashiorkor: weight-for-age is 60–80% of expected. Marasmus: weight-for-age is <60% — much more severe.
Marasmus typically presents in infants <1 year. Kwashiorkor presents at 1–3 years (after weaning to starchy diet).
BOTH have high mortality risk due to immune dysfunction. Kwashiorkor: higher infection risk due to edema and skin breakdown. Marasmus: high if superinfection occurs.

name: RTA Triad
subtitle: 10 features · Type I vs Type II vs Type IV

features.explanation: Type I RTA: inability to secrete H+ in distal tubule → urine pH remains >5.5 even during systemic acidosis. Pathognomonic for distal RTA.
features.trap: Urine pH >5.5 despite acidosis = Type I RTA. This is the single most important discriminator.

features.explanation: Type II RTA: urine pH is variable. When HCO3- is depleted, urine pH is <5.5. When replete, it rises >5.5 because the proximal tubule cannot reabsorb the filtered bicarbonate.
Type IV RTA: the distal tubule CAN acidify urine (pH <5.5). The problem is aldosterone deficiency/resistance → hyperkalaemia → impaired ammoniagenesis.
Types I and II BOTH cause hypokalaemia. Type I: K+ wasting in distal tubule. Type II: K+ wasting secondary to massive bicarbonaturia and volume depletion.
Type IV RTA causes HYPERkalaemia — this is the key differentiator. Aldosterone deficiency → reduced distal K+ secretion. NEVER give potassium citrate in Type IV.
Type I RTA: citrate loss + alkaline urine → calcium phosphate stone formation and nephrocalcinosis. Types II and IV do NOT have this complication.
Type II RTA: FE HCO3- >15% when plasma HCO3- is above threshold. Type I: FE HCO3- <5%. This is a key lab differentiator.
Type II RTA is associated with Fanconi syndrome — generalized proximal tubule dysfunction causing glycosuria, aminoaciduria, and phosphaturia. Type I is NOT associated with Fanconi.
Amphotericin B damages the distal tubule H+ pump → causes Type I RTA. Lithium causes nephrogenic DI. Acetazolamide causes Type II RTA (inhibits proximal carbonic anhydrase).
Type IV RTA due to aldosterone deficiency responds to fludrocortisone 0.1–0.3 mg/day. Types I and II do NOT respond to fludrocortisone.

name: DI Duel
subtitle: 8 features · Central vs Nephrogenic Diabetes Insipidus

features.explanation: Central DI: ADH (vasopressin) is LOW or undetectable due to pituitary/hypothalamic damage (craniopharyngioma, trauma, surgery, histiocytosis).
Nephrogenic DI: ADH level is normal or elevated, but the collecting duct is resistant to its action. The problem is in the kidney, not the pituitary.
Central DI: desmopressin (DDAVP) causes urine osmolality to rise >50% because the collecting duct is intact and responsive once ADH is replaced.
features.trap: Fluid deprivation test + DDAVP response is the gold standard. Central = responds. Nephrogenic = does NOT respond.

features.text: NO change in urine osmolality after desmopressin
features.explanation: Nephrogenic DI: DDAVP has NO effect because the collecting duct V2 receptors or AQP2 channels are defective or resistant.

features.text: Absent posterior pituitary bright spot on MRI
features.explanation: Central DI: MRI shows absent posterior pituitary bright spot (the normal T1 hyperintensity from stored ADH). Sensitivity ~80%. Nephrogenic DI has a normal pituitary MRI.
Craniopharyngioma is the most common CNS tumor causing central DI in children. Always do MRI pituitary in suspected central DI.
Congenital nephrogenic DI: 90% are X-linked AVPR2 mutations (males affected). 10% are AQP2 mutations. Central DI is usually acquired, not genetic.

features.text: Lithium as most common drug cause
features.explanation: Lithium is the most common drug-induced nephrogenic DI. It enters collecting duct principal cells via ENaC and inhibits glycogen synthase kinase-3beta, reducing AQP2 expression.

name: Adrenal Crisis
subtitle: 8 features · Primary vs Secondary Adrenal Insufficiency

features.explanation: Primary AI: ACTH is elevated because cortisol deficiency removes negative feedback on the pituitary. ACTH also cross-reacts with MSH receptors → hyperpigmentation.
Secondary AI: ACTH is LOW because the pituitary cannot produce it (craniopharyngioma, surgery, trauma, radiation, congenital hypopituitarism).
Hyperpigmentation of skin and mucous membranes is present in primary AI due to ACTH/MSH cross-reactivity. ABSENT in secondary AI — this is the fastest clinical differentiator.
features.trap: NO hyperpigmentation + NO hyperkalaemia = secondary AI. These two features are the most tested differentiators.

features.explanation: Secondary AI: NO hyperpigmentation. The child may appear pale. MSH is also low because ACTH is low.
Primary AI: hyperkalaemia is present because aldosterone is deficient (zona glomerulosa destroyed). RAAS cannot compensate.
Secondary AI: potassium is NORMAL because aldosterone production is independent of ACTH — the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system remains intact.
Primary AI: salt craving is present due to aldosterone deficiency → sodium loss → volume depletion. Absent in secondary AI.
Primary AI requires BOTH hydrocortisone AND fludrocortisone (mineralocorticoid). Secondary AI usually needs only hydrocortisone — fludrocortisone is usually NOT needed.

name: Hyponatremia Wars
subtitle: 8 features · SIADH vs Cerebral Salt Wasting

features.explanation: SIADH: euvolaemic. Total body sodium is normal but water is retained inappropriately. No signs of volume depletion or overload.
CSW: hypovolaemic. Both sodium AND water are lost from the kidneys → true volume depletion. This is the KEY differentiator from SIADH.
features.trap: SIADH and CSW both have high urine sodium and hyponatraemia. The KEY distinction is volume status: SIADH = euvolaemic; CSW = hypovolaemic. Treating CSW with fluid restriction causes shock.

features.explanation: SIADH is treated with fluid restriction — the cornerstone of management. Restricting fluids in CSW would worsen hypovolaemia and cause shock.
CSW requires aggressive sodium replacement (may need 3% saline) + fludrocortisone. Fluid restriction is CONTRAINDICATED — it worsens hypovolaemia.
SIADH: urine output is low or normal despite hyponatraemia. The kidneys inappropriately retain water.
CSW: urine output is high because the kidneys are wasting both sodium and water.
SIADH: haematocrit is normal or low (dilutional). CSW: haematocrit is elevated (haemoconcentration from volume depletion).
CSW: BUN and creatinine are elevated due to prerenal azotaemia from volume depletion. SIADH: BUN/creatinine are low/normal.
